---
title: "Data Sources"
description: "Every source behind the site, what it is used for, and its licence"
---

Everything on DataCan comes from an authoritative primary source — a national
statistical agency, a government department, an international organization, or
an established research institution. We never use Wikipedia or third-party
compilations, even as a cross-check. Every chart carries its own source note;
this page is the full roster.

## Canadian official sources

- **[Statistics Canada](https://www.statcan.gc.ca/)** — the backbone of the
  site. Population estimates and components, the 2021 Census (diversity,
  religion, incomes, and home values down to the census-tract and
  dissemination-area level), the Consumer Price Index, the New Housing Price
  Index, incomes and low income, food insecurity, labour force series, housing
  starts and rental vacancy (CMHC data published through Statistics Canada),
  electricity generation, university tuition, crime (the Uniform Crime
  Reporting survey, the Crime Severity Index, and hate crime), substance use
  (the Canadian Community Health Survey), government finance and employment,
  and merchandise trade. Used under the [Statistics Canada Open Licence](https://www.statcan.gc.ca/en/reference/licence).
- **[Environment and Climate Change Canada](https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change.html)** —
  greenhouse-gas emissions (the National Inventory Report), the Canadian
  Environmental Sustainability Indicators (temperature change, air-quality
  concentrations), the Air Pollutant Emissions Inventory, raw air-quality
  monitoring from the National Air Pollution Surveillance program (back to
  1974), homogenized long-run city temperatures (AHCCD), and regional warming
  trends. Open Government Licence – Canada.
- **[Public Health Agency of Canada](https://health-infobase.canada.ca/)** —
  the Substance-Related Harms dataset behind the Substance Use page: apparent
  opioid- and stimulant-toxicity deaths and hospitalizations, national and by
  province, updated quarterly. Open Government Licence – Canada.
- **[Bank of Canada](https://www.bankofcanada.ca/valet/docs)** — the policy
  rate back to 1935, mortgage rates, bond yields, and the exchange rate, via
  the Valet API. Reproduced with attribution as the Bank requires.
- **[Natural Resources Canada](https://natural-resources.canada.ca/)** — the
  Canadian National Fire Database (wildfire), the Atlas of Canada (major lakes
  and rivers, permafrost), elevation and named peaks, and the hillshade
  basemap on the Elevation page. Open Government Licence – Canada.
- **[Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat](https://www.canada.ca/en/treasury-board-secretariat.html) and
  [GC InfoBase](https://www.tbs-sct.canada.ca/ems-sgd/edb-bdd/index-eng.html)** —
  federal public-service headcounts and demographics, and federal spending by
  department and standard object. Open Government Licence – Canada.
- **[Canadian Institute for Health Information](https://www.cihi.ca/)** —
  surgical wait times (the share of patients treated within the pan-Canadian
  benchmark), updated annually from CIHI's published data tables.
- **[Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada](https://agriculture.canada.ca/)** — the
  National Ecological Framework behind the ecozone maps.
- **[Toronto Police Service](https://data.torontopolice.on.ca/)** — the
  neighbourhood crime map and crime-seasonality charts on the Crime & Safety
  page. Open Government Licence – Ontario.

## International sources

- **[OECD](https://data-explorer.oecd.org/)** — the engine of the peer
  comparisons: GDP per capita and growth, productivity, jobs, house prices,
  household debt and disposable income, inequality and poverty, wages,
  government debt, deficits, revenue and interest costs, life expectancy,
  health spending and system capacity (beds, physicians, nurses, MRI units),
  R&D and researchers, fine-particle air pollution, CO₂, and
  general-government employment. Accessed through the OECD's SDMX API.
- **[World Bank](https://data.worldbank.org/)** — peer comparisons sourced
  from WHO, UN, SIPRI, and IDF data: business investment, defence spending,
  suicide, overweight, tobacco and alcohol use, diabetes, maternal mortality,
  universal-health-coverage index, and out-of-pocket health spending. CC BY 4.0.
- **[Our World in Data](https://ourworldindata.org/)** — the energy mix
  (compiled from the Energy Institute, Ember, and the U.S. EIA) and
  consumption-based CO₂ (Global Carbon Project). CC BY.
- **[World Happiness Report](https://worldhappiness.report/)** — life
  evaluations from the Gallup World Poll and the factors that statistically
  account for them.
- **[National Snow and Ice Data Center](https://nsidc.org/data/seaice_index)** —
  the Sea Ice Index behind the Arctic sea-ice extent chart.

## One charts-only exception

- **[CREA (Canadian Real Estate Association)](https://www.crea.ca/)** — the
  MLS® Home Price Index series on the Housing page are published as **charts
  only**, with CREA's written permission for educational use. Unlike every
  other source on this site, the underlying CREA data is not redistributed and
  is not available in our downloads. Each of those charts carries CREA's
  attribution line.

## Licences, in short

Canadian government sources are used under the
[Open Government Licence – Canada](https://open.canada.ca/en/open-government-licence-canada)
(or the equivalent Statistics Canada and Ontario licences); the World Bank and
Our World in Data are CC BY; the OECD permits reproduction with attribution,
as does the Bank of Canada. Everything except the CREA series may be
redistributed, and our cleaned CSVs are free to
[download from GitHub](https://github.com/CanadaObservatory/CanadaDataObs).

## How fresh is each series?

Most series refresh automatically every week, picking up whatever the source
agency has published — monthly for the CPI, quarterly for population, annually
for most OECD series. A few sources have no machine-readable feed and are
rebuilt manually about once a year when their agency publishes (CIHI wait
times, the by-city air-quality history, the substance-use survey, detailed
crime tables). Census-based maps are rebuilt once per census; their charts say
so. Every chart shows the date of its most recent observation.
